risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients who provide written informed consent will be randomly allocated to either the optimised bowel care programme (control group) or the optimised bowel care programme and massage (intervention group). Randomisation will utilise a web-based randomisation system at the Tayside Clinical Trials Unit. Randomisation will be stratified by site and minimised on level of disability (walking unaided, walking aided or wheelchair bound)." Comment: The study describes a centralized web-based randomisation system with stratification and minimisation, which indicates a proper random allocation process rather than a predictable or non-random sequence.

Quote: "Randomisation will utilise a web-based randomisation system at the Tayside Clinical Trials Unit." Comment: A web-based central randomisation system indicates allocation was concealed from recruiting staff, preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention, participants and the research team delivering the intervention will not be blinded to the treatment received." Comment: This was an open-label trial for participants and intervention staff, and the primary and secondary outcomes were mainly self-reported bowel symptoms, quality of life, and diaries, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation; therefore performance bias is high.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention, participants and the research team delivering the intervention will not be blinded to the treatment received. Outcome measures are primarily self-reported and submitted anonymously. Those involved in the data analyses and statistics will be blinded to the group allocation." Comment: The main outcomes were assessed by participant self-report questionnaires and diaries, so the participant is effectively the outcome assessor. Because participants were not blinded, knowledge of allocation could influence reporting of constipation, bowel symptoms, QoL, and related outcomes. Blinding of data analysts does not protect against detection bias in self-reported outcomes.

Quote: "Analysis will be performed for the intention-to-treat population" and "The extent of missing data will be explored... If necessary, multiple imputations will be utilised to impute missing data assuming the missingness mechanism is missing at random (MAR)." Comment: Although this is a protocol, it shows a planned ITT analysis with an appropriate missing-data strategy. The study also anticipated 20% drop-out and increased the sample size to allow for greater than expected attrition, suggesting attrition was considered and managed rather than likely to bias results.
